description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: Considering the challenges associated with bias-temperature instability (NBTI) in p-MOSFETs on (110) oriented silicon wafers and the use of three-dimensional FinFET structures on (100) wafers, how does wafer orientation influence NBTI degradation and what design strategies can be employed in FinFET fabrication to mitigate these reliability issues?

gold_context_sources.evidence: Most ICs are fabricated on (100) oriented Si wafers. However, it has been known since 1968 that hole mobility is higher for p-MOSFETs on (110) oriented wafers with the channel in the (110) direction. Along with the higher hole mobility, however, is a higher interface trap density. Dy has been reported as D;(100)=7.7x10 and Dy(110)=14x10 cm2eV. Hence one would expect more severe NBTI degradation on (110) oriented wafers, as indeed has been observed. For certain three-dimensional devices, e.g., FinFETs, when fabricated on (100) wafers with the channel in the conventional (110) direction, the vertical sidewalls are (110) oriented, leading to NBTI problems. However, forming FinFETs on (100) wafers with the channel in the (100) direction, leads to (100) vertical sidewalls.

gold_context_sources.section_title: 1. INTRODUCTION - A. Process Flow - IV. CONCLUSIONS
gold_context_sources.evidence: For the 3 um pitch designs, the best Kelvin yield is >95% and daisy chain yield >90%; for the 2 um pitch design, the Kelvin e-yield is >85% and daisy chain yield is >70%. The comparison of 3 um pitch results show that better e-yields are from the square pads (design B and design C), although the overlay tolerance is more forgiving for the rectangle pads (design D). Therefore, the Cu bulge-out probably outweighs the placement accuracy in this case. Comparison between design B and design C shows that a larger top pad is more favored as it is expected to have higher Cu bulge out and thus better Cu-Cu bonding.

ground_truth_answer: Wafer orientation significantly influences NBTI degradation in p-MOSFETs. Specifically, p-MOSFETs fabricated on (110) oriented silicon wafers with channels aligned in the (110) direction exhibit higher hole mobility compared to those on (100) wafers; however, this advantage comes at the cost of a higher interface trap density (Dy(110) is nearly double that of Dy(100)), which leads to more severe NBTI degradation. This degradation has been empirically observed and is problematic for devices utilizing (110) wafers. In three-dimensional FinFET devices fabricated on (100) wafers, the orientation of the channel direction also impacts NBTI reliability: FinFETs with channels oriented in the conventional (110) direction have vertical sidewalls that are (110) oriented, inheriting the NBTI issues associated with the (110) interface. Conversely, by orienting the FinFET channels along the (100) direction on (100) wafers, the vertical sidewalls become (100) oriented, which mitigates the NBTI reliability concerns. Therefore, a key design strategy to reduce NBTI degradation in FinFETs is to carefully select the channel direction to ensure that the vertical sidewalls correspond to the (100) orientation rather than (110). This approach balances performance benefits with reliability by leveraging the lower interface trap density and associated lower NBTI degradation of the (100) surfaces. Additionally, in die-to-wafer hybrid bonding for advanced nodes (2–3 µm pitch), design choices such as pad shape and size influence bonding yield and reliability; larger top pads with higher copper bulge-out improve Cu-Cu bonding quality, which can indirectly support device reliability. Overall, wafer orientation dictates the interface quality impacting NBTI, and careful orientation and device design can mitigate degradation effects.
